behaviors.description: Exponential backoff delay doubles on each consecutive failure and caps at configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This can fail inside one pure delay-calculation boundary (e.g., attempt count + config -> delay), without module handoff. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: No real OS/network/subprocess is required; fake time values are enough. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: Not AI-related. Rejection-gate: I considered Integration, but behavior text is only about delay math ('doubles... up to a configured maximum'), which does not require cross-module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff function directly with attempt sequence inputs and max cap config; assert returned delays follow 2x growth and stop increasing after cap.

behaviors.description: Retry logic detects rate-limit responses and computes the next wait time correctly.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Failure is at module boundary between response classification and retry scheduler/backoff policy. REAL-INFRA: Can be reproduced with in-process stubbed HTTP responses; no real network needed. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real OS/network/subprocess dependency in behavior text; (b)(c)(d) no AI stages. Rejection-gate: I considered System due to REST context, but behavior text only requires 'identifies a rate-limit response and computes wait time'—this is verifiable with deterministic local response fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Wire client retry module with a stub transport returning a 429-style response (and optional Retry-After metadata), then assert classifier marks it retriable and scheduler computes expected delay.

behaviors.description: After waiting, client resends the original request and returns successful result transparently.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Real failure appears in interaction between timer/backoff, request replay, and response propagation modules. Unit is too low because it would miss handoff bugs (wrong payload resent, result not bubbled). REAL-INFRA: Fake clock + stub transport can catch this without real HTTP. Rejection-gate: I considered System, but behavior text does not require real network conditions; it requires resend semantics and transparent return.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a scripted in-process transport (first response=rate-limited, second=success). Assert second call reuses original request parameters and final `call()` resolves to success payload with no retry artifacts exposed.

behaviors.description: After max retries, client throws typed `QuotaExhaustedError` instead of generic error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This depends on retry counter + termination + error-mapping interaction, not just one function. REAL-INFRA: In-process repeated rate-limit stubs are sufficient. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network requirement in behavior text; no AI dependence; no multi-agent chain. Rejection-gate: I considered Unit (pure error mapping), but behavior explicitly conditions on 'after configured maximum number of retries', which is orchestration state across modules.
behaviors.test_description: Configure small max retry count, stub transport to always return rate-limit failure, execute `call()`, assert rejection is instance/type `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata, and not a generic error class.

behaviors.description: Full retry chain terminates correctly and never loops indefinitely before surfacing exhaustion error.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Infinite-loop risk emerges from interaction of retry loop control, backoff scheduling, and quota tracking state. Unit tests of pieces can miss non-terminating composition. REAL-INFRA: Deterministic fake clock and stub transport can expose loop/non-termination without real network/processes. Rejection-gate: I considered System for timing realism, but behavior text targets logical termination of 'full retry chain' rather than OS scheduler/network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run client with fake timers and always-rate-limited stub transport; advance time through all scheduled retries; assert call settles (rejects) within bounded virtual time/attempt count and no additional retries are scheduled afterward.

behaviors.description: Concurrent async callers in same process consume one shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Failure occurs at shared-state handoff across concurrent callers and quota manager; single-function tests miss race and isolation bugs. REAL-INFRA: Can be reproduced with in-process concurrency (Promises) and stub transport; no real network/OS primitives required by behavior text. Rejection-gate: I considered System due to concurrency timing, but text constrains scope to 'within the same process', which is directly testable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota manager; launch multiple concurrent `call()` invocations against scripted responses; assert aggregate token/retry accounting reflects one shared bucket (not per-caller independent quotas).

behaviors.description: Queued waiting requests are dispatched FIFO by original submission order.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Ordering bugs appear at queue + dispatcher + quota-unblock interaction boundary, not in isolated functions alone. REAL-INFRA: In-process queue with controlled clock and stub transport is enough; real HTTP/OS not required. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no OS/network necessity, no AI output dependence, no multi-agent chain. Rejection-gate: I considered System for scheduler jitter, but behavior text specifies logical order guarantee ('order they were originally submitted'), which deterministic integration harness can validate.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple calls quickly while quota forces waiting; release capacity stepwise; assert underlying transport dispatch sequence matches submission order and returned promises resolve consistently with FIFO dispatch.
